kpi_cards.label: LME CASH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: Mon 13-Jul OFFICIAL (Westmetall): $3,138.50, −$17.50 (−0.55%) vs Fri — the FIRST official struck after the IRGC closure declaration, and it FELL. It printed 13:20 London, ~4½ hours BEFORE Trump's blockade-plus-20%-toll announcement (~17:53 London). After hours the metal firmed: LME 3M closed $3,158 (SMM) and Asia traded ~$3,200 this morning. ~18.6% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855).

kpi_cards.label: LME 3-MONTH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 13-Jul official $3,147.00 (−$6.00, −0.19%). The week's key diagnostic answered 'not yet': the cash–3M spread SNAPPED BACK to −$8.50 CONTANGO from Friday's +$3 backwardation — the ring did not reprice the blockade. Resistance stack unchanged: $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382 of the whole decline); $3,050 remains the structural line below.

kpi_cards.label: LME STOCK (t)
kpi_cards.delta: 13-Jul official 286,225 t — a further −1,500 t. The drawdown is unbroken since early April (509,250 t on 02-Jan → 286,225, −43.8% YTD): the thinnest visible cover of this cycle, now facing a transit artery for ~25% of ex-China supply that is contested by BOTH sides — an Iranian closure declaration and a US blockade-plus-toll regime.

kpi_cards.label: BRENT ($/bbl)
kpi_cards.delta: Brent settled +9.6% at $83.30 Monday — its best single day since May-2020 (CNBC) — after the toll announcement, tanker strikes inside Omani waters and a hit on a Kuwaiti offshore platform; holding ~$83 Tuesday morning (Trading Economics $83.37, +9.68% on the day). WTI +9.4% to $78.14. Energy is the largest smelter cost line — the oil–metal pricing gap is now the widest of this crisis.

benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: Mon 13-Jul OFFICIAL (Westmetall) — the first official after the IRGC declared the strait closed: $3,138.50 vs 10-Jul $3,156.00 = −$17.50 (−0.55%). The ring prints 13:20 London; Trump's blockade-plus-toll post landed ~17:53 London, AFTER this print — so Monday's official carries the closure news but NOT the toll. avg = June-2026 monthly mean ($3,458.6).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 13-Jul official $3,147.00 vs 10-Jul $3,153.00 (−$6.00, −0.19%). The later evening close was $3,158 (SMM) and Asia traded ~$3,200 this morning after the toll headline — today's 13:20 official is the first to carry the full two-sided-blockade news. Resistance $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382); $3,050 re-opens the downside. avg = June-2026 3M mean ($3,438.9).

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: CONTANGO (−$8.50): cash $3,138.50 under 3M $3,147.00. The diagnostic set up last week has answered: Friday's +$3 backwardation did NOT widen on the first post-blockade official — it snapped back to contango, i.e. the physical front end did not reprice the closure at ring time. Caveat: the toll announcement post-dates the print; whether TODAY'S official rebuilds the backwardation is now the tell.

premiums.name: MJP Q3 (Main Japanese Port)
premiums.src: Q3-2026 SETTLED at $395/t (S&P Global Platts, assessed 06-Jul) — a decade high (+12% q/q vs Q2's $350–353). Spot has printed BELOW settlement ($355, SMM 10-Jul). The two-sided blockade — IRGC closure plus the new US 20% transit toll — is what could arrest that slide before Q4 talks (~Sep). Source: Platts via IndexBox. avg = Q1-26 settlement ($195).

premiums.name: CIF Japan spot (MJP spot)
premiums.src: Latest public SMM weekly print (10-Jul): $355/t, down from $380 — assessed BEFORE the closure declaration, the tanker strikes AND the US toll. Friday 17-Jul's weekly is the first print to carry all three. Verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0343 / SMM.

premiums.name: European Duty Unpaid (EDU)
premiums.src: Platts daily P1020A duty-UNPAID in-whs Rotterdam last publicly assessed $525–560/t (mid $542.50, 03-Jul) — no fresher public print. Structural floor: 6% EU duty + CBAM + Mozal (~580 ktpa) on care & maintenance. Gulf war-risk freight is re-widening and a 20% US toll on strait cargo adds a brand-new delivered-cost leg to any Gulf-origin unit (S&P Global / SMM).

premiums.name: Billets Rotterdam Duty-Paid
premiums.src: Value-added 6063 billet conversion premium, in-whs duty-paid Rotterdam — LAST PUBLIC REFERENCE (late-Jun; no fresh public print). Conversion premia are stickier than ingot premia; Mozal remains down. Verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0002.

premiums.name: MW US Transaction (Platts)
premiums.src: Platts US MW Transaction premium last publicly assessed 108.7¢/lb ≈ $2,396/t (03-Jul). The tariff-$ leg moves MECHANICALLY with the LME (≈$1,569/t at the 13-Jul cash of $3,138.5 × 50%), so any blockade-driven LME gap lifts the US all-in automatically. SMM's 10-Jul weekly had DDP at 109.75¢. Verify S&P Global.

premiums.name: US Midwest Duty-Paid (P1020A)
premiums.src: SMM weekly DDP print 109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul, from 110.25¢) — assessed before the weekend. Duty-paid = LME + Section-232 tariff (50%) + market premium; the policy leg is ~two-thirds of the all-in. A 20% strait toll, if it sticks, is a further policy-driven cost line for any Gulf-origin unit landing in the US. Verify Platts/Fastmarkets.

macro.name: Brent crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: Brent settled +9.6% at $83.30 Monday — the biggest daily gain since May-2020 (CNBC) — after Trump reinstated the Iranian blockade with a 20% cargo toll and Iranian missiles hit two tankers and a Kuwaiti offshore platform. Trading Economics shows $83.37 (+9.68%); Tuesday morning it is holding ~$83 (−1% intraday). OPEC separately cut 2026 demand growth to 800 kb/d. Energy is the largest smelter cost line — and oil-led CPI risk is now the rates story.

macro.name: US Dollar Index (DXY)
macro.note: DXY ~101.2 and firm (+0.3% Tuesday morning) — risk-off plus HAWKISH Fed repricing: Governor Waller said a hot core print this week would force the Fed to consider a NEAR-TERM HIKE. A firmer dollar is a direct headwind for dollar-priced metals even as the supply story turns more bullish; the two forces continue to offset.

macro.name: EUR/USD
macro.note: Euro ~1.1390 (−0.2%). The ECB tightened in June explicitly on war-driven energy costs; an $83 Brent and a formally two-sided Hormuz keep that energy-inflation channel wide open and argue against any near-term reversal.

macro.name: USD/CNY
macro.note: Yuan steady ~6.78, supported by a blowout June trade report: exports +27% y/y (fastest since Oct-2021, vs +18.5% consensus) and imports +36% (vs +24%) — AI-hardware demand plus tariff front-running. H1 trade +16.9%. June's unwrought-aluminium detail is pending; May ran 632 kt (+15.5% y/y), Jan–May 2.685 Mt (+10.4%) — the export overhang that has been capping LME rallies (GACC via CNBC/SCMP).

macro.name: USD/JPY
macro.note: Yen ~162.4 (weaker on the rate-differential re-pricing). A weak yen marginally cushions Japan-bound cargo premia in local terms — but the binding Q4-talks variable is whether Gulf metal can transit Hormuz at all, and at what toll.

macro.name: US 10Y yield (%)
macro.note: 10Y ~4.62% (+6 bp) into TODAY'S June CPI (13:30 London): consensus headline −0.1% m/m (~3.9% y/y, down from 4.2%) on June's ~10% gasoline slide, core ~+0.2% m/m / ~2.9% y/y. The catch: July's oil spike is NOT in this report — a soft headline may mislead, and Waller has pre-committed the Fed to a hike discussion if core runs hot (IG / Kiplinger / Cleveland Fed nowcast).

macro_events: BLOCKADE MEETS COUNTER-BLOCKADE: Trump reinstated 'THE IRANIAN BLOCKADE' — only Iranian ships/customers barred — and set a 20% toll on all other strait cargo ('reimbursement for providing safety and security'); the US military begins enforcing at 16:00 ET TODAY (14-Jul). Iran says it will never accept US control of the strait and warns the region would be 'engulfed in the flames of war' (NPR / Truth Social / SMM).

THE SAFE LANE WAS HIT: Iranian cruise missiles struck UAE tankers Mombasa and Al Bahiyah inside OMANI territorial waters on Monday — one Indian seafarer killed, eight injured — and a Kuwaiti offshore drilling platform was damaged, the first direct energy-infrastructure hit in weeks. Kpler: strait crossings down >50% w/w; ~230 loaded tankers idling inside the Gulf (UAE MoD / CNBC / TheStreet).

OIL'S BEST DAY SINCE 2020, METAL'S SHRUG: Brent settled +9.6% at $83.30 (WTI +9.4%) while aluminium's first post-blockade official FELL 0.55% to $3,138.5 and the front spread snapped back to −$8.50 contango. The metal firmed only after hours ($3,158 close; ~$3,200 in Asia). Today's 13:20 London official is the first to carry the toll (CNBC / Westmetall / SMM).

CHINA JUNE TRADE BLOWOUT (released this morning): exports +27% y/y to $412bn — the fastest since Oct-2021 and far above the +18.5% consensus; imports +36% (vs +24%). H1 trade +16.9%. Aluminium-specific June detail is pending; May unwrought+semis ran 632 kt (+15.5%). Strong exports = the supply-side counterweight to the war story (GACC via CNBC / SCMP).

THE WEEK'S HARD DATES: US June CPI TODAY 13:30 London (headline seen −0.1% m/m; Waller: hot core → near-term hike consideration); Rio Tinto Q2 production report mid-week; Alcoa Q2 Thu 16-Jul after the NYSE close (consensus EPS $2.24, revenue ~$4.12bn); Fri 17-Jul: US Treasury Iranian-oil wind-down deadline AND the first post-blockade premium weeklies (SMM/Platts).
lme_commentary: Monday finally put a metal print against the blockade — and the metal blinked the other way. The first LME official struck after the IRGC's closure declaration came in LOWER: cash $3,138.50 (−0.55%), 3M $3,147.00 (−0.19%), and the cash–3M spread — whose direction was flagged here as the single most informative print of the week — snapped BACK to a −$8.50 contango from Friday's +$3 backwardation. At ring time, the physical front end declined to reprice a declared closure. The sequence matters, though: the ring closed at 13:20 London, Trump's blockade-plus-20%-toll post landed around 17:53, the evening close printed $3,158, and Asia carried the metal to ~$3,200 overnight — so today's official is the first that can price the full two-sided regime. Around the flat price, the physical picture is genuinely two-handed. Bullish: stock drew a further 1,500 t to 286,225 (−43.8% YTD, the thinnest cover of the cycle); the southern Omani corridor — the one workaround — was hit by cruise missiles (one seafarer killed); Kpler counts strait crossings down more than half with ~230 loaded tankers trapped; and Brent just had its best day since May-2020. Bearish: China's June exports beat by nine points (+27% y/y), every overseas premium print last week was lower, EGA's alumina restart is ramping, and Waller has put a near-term Fed HIKE on the table if today's core CPI runs hot — a dollar headwind aimed straight at metals. Technical map unchanged: $3,230 (MA20) then $3,260 (.382) above — a CLOSING break upgrades the count toward $3,330–3,400; $3,050 is the structural line below. Watch two prints today: the 13:20 official (does the spread rebuild toward backwardation?) and 13:30 CPI (does the core hand the dollar a hike?).

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$340/t FOB East Australia — latest public reference. The global surplus story is intact (EGA's 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah refinery restarted 10-Jul; Nalco guides $320–330 for 2026) — but the DELIVERED leg into the Gulf worsened again: missiles hit tankers inside the Omani corridor and a 20% US toll now applies to strait cargo. Verify SMM/Fastmarkets.

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region ~$304/t — easing on the building surplus (latest public reference). SMM flags ex-China metallurgical-grade output down ~6% y/y in June on geopolitical and energy pressure — a partial offset to the EGA restart. Verify Platts.

alumina.name: SHFE alumina futures (AO2609)
alumina.src: Last public futures print in the ¥2,700s (SMM); price below all moving averages, port stocks building. SMM's domestic spot weighted average slipped ~¥61 to ~¥2,552/t by 14-Jul. Two-sided Hormuz restriction squeezes GULF refinery feed (~60% of regional alumina imports transit the strait) even as the global balance stays long (SMM).

alumina.name: Platts FOB Australia (futures)
alumina.src: Alumina FOB Australia (Platts) futures ~$309/t — CME/TradingView; no material move in the latest public print.

alumina_notes: The alumina market remains the mirror image of metal: globally LOOSENING (EGA's 2.4 Mtpa restart, Guinea bauxite oversupply at ~$68/dmt CIF China, Chinese port stocks building; domestic China spot ~¥2,552 and drifting) — ex-China consolidation in the $300–340 band still holds.

The regional wrinkle sharpened this week: with the strait contested by BOTH sides and missiles landing inside the Omani corridor, the ~60% of Gulf alumina imports that transit Hormuz face higher war-risk cover AND a 20% US toll — a regional feed squeeze inside a global surplus. SMM separately flags ex-China metallurgical-grade output down ~6% y/y in June.

Guinea's pending export-quota policy (targeting >$100/dmt bauxite) remains the one clear bullish catalyst on the commodity leg; Metro Mining's record 1.8 Mt Q2 shipments underline how long the seaborne market currently is.
net_read: THE FIRST METAL VERDICT WAS 'NO' — BUT IT PRE-DATED THE TOLL. Oil repriced a two-sided blockade with its best day since 2020 (+9.6% to $83.30); aluminium's first post-blockade official FELL to $3,138.5 and the front spread snapped back to −$8.50 contango — the physical front end declined to panic. Since that print: a 20% US toll on strait cargo, missiles inside the Omani corridor, a $3,158 close and ~$3,200 in Asia. Net: the burden of proof now sits on TODAY'S 13:20 official and 13:30 CPI — a rebuilt backwardation plus a tame core validates the catch-up trade toward $3,230/$3,260; a second contango print plus a hot core (Waller: near-term hike) says the adequately-supplied tape and the dollar still cap this market. Two-handed by construction; the dates resolve it this week.

feed.text: EUROPEAN MORNING CHECK: Westmetall has tabled Monday's officials — Al cash $3,138.50 (−0.55%) / 3M $3,147.00 (−0.19%) / stock 286,225 t (−1,500) — the FIRST official after the closure declaration, and the cash–3M spread snapped back to −$8.50 CONTANGO. After the ring: LME 3M closed $3,158 (SMM) and Asia traded ~$3,200 after the toll headline. SHFE 2608 settled ¥23,075 (+0.33%); SMM advises core ranges of ¥22,600–23,400 / $3,100–3,200.

CHINA JUNE TRADE BLOWOUT: exports +27% y/y (fastest since Oct-2021, vs +18.5% consensus), imports +36% (vs +24%); H1 +16.9%. Bearish for the flat price via the export overhang — June's unwrought-aluminium detail pending (May: 632 kt, +15.5%). Domestic ingot inventory kept destocking: 1.078 Mt in mainstream consumption areas, −52 kt vs last Monday (GACC via CNBC / SMM).

TRUMP REINSTATES THE BLOCKADE + 20% TOLL (~17:53 London, AFTER the LME ring and close): only Iranian ships/customers barred; all other cargo pays 20% (~$32m for a loaded supertanker vs Iran's former ≤$2m). US military enforcement begins 16:00 ET Tue. Iran: the region would be 'engulfed in the flames of war' if the conflict widens (NPR / SMM).

SOUTHERN CORRIDOR HIT: Iranian cruise missiles struck UAE tankers Mombasa and Al Bahiyah INSIDE Omani territorial waters — one Indian crew member killed, eight injured (UAE MoD). A Kuwaiti offshore drilling platform was also damaged. The one functioning workaround now carries demonstrated missile risk; war-risk quotes widen before any premium print moves.

OIL: Brent settled +9.6% at $83.30 — best single day since May-2020 (CNBC); WTI +9.4% to $78.14; EU TTF gas +8% to ~€53. Kpler: Hormuz crossings down >50% w/w, ~230 loaded tankers waiting inside the Gulf. OPEC cut 2026 demand growth to 800 kb/d — a partial offset.

THE METAL'S OWN PRINT WAS SOFT: the 13:20 London official — the first after the closure declaration — fell 0.55% (cash $3,138.5) and the front spread flipped back to contango (−$8.50). The physical front end did not confirm the war story at ring time; today's official is the first that can price the toll.

SECONDARY/SCRAP TIGHTENING (SMM): the UAE implemented an aluminium scrap EXPORT BAN and EU tariff changes tighten seaborne scrap further; China's reverse-invoicing suspension (Shandong, from July) is cutting compliant domestic scrap supply — a cost-side support building under secondary alloys (ADC12 ¥ down ~100 on demand weakness regardless).

news.headline: TWO-SIDED BLOCKADE: Trump reinstated 'THE IRANIAN BLOCKADE' and set a 20% toll on all other strait cargo, with US military enforcement from 16:00 ET 14-Jul; Iran — which itself declared the strait closed on the 12th — vows never to accept US control and warns of a region 'engulfed in the flames of war'. Kpler: crossings −50%+ w/w; ~230 loaded tankers idling inside the Gulf. The transit artery for ~25% of ex-China aluminium supply is now restricted by BOTH belligerents.
news.source: NPR (13-Jul) — npr.org/2026/07/13/nx-s1-5891746; SMM morning minutes (14-Jul); TheStreet/Kpler

news.headline: THE SAFE LANE ISN'T: UAE tankers Mombasa and Al Bahiyah were hit by Iranian cruise missiles inside OMANI territorial waters — one Indian seafarer killed, eight injured (UAE MoD) — hours after the JMIC had called the southern corridor operational. Oman is circulating a two-corridor traffic proposal (southern free, northern under Iranian approval), not yet agreed. War-risk cover re-prices immediately; the 20% toll stacks on top.
news.source: UAE Ministry of Defence via BOE Report / BusinessToday (13/14-Jul); CNN live (13-Jul)

news.headline: OIL BELIEVED IT, THE METAL DIDN'T (YET): Brent's +9.6% Monday was its best day since May-2020 — while aluminium's first post-blockade official FELL 0.55% to $3,138.5 and the cash–3M spread snapped back to −$8.50 CONTANGO. The metal firmed only after the ring ($3,158 close; ~$3,200 in Asia, post-toll). Today's 13:20 London official is the first that can price the full regime.

CHINA'S EXPORT MACHINE ACCELERATED INTO THE BLOCKADE: June exports +27% y/y (fastest since Oct-2021; consensus +18.5%), imports +36%; H1 trade +16.9%. Aluminium June detail pending (May: 632 kt unwrought+semis, +15.5% y/y; Jan–May 2.685 Mt, +10.4%). Domestic ingot destocking continues (1.078 Mt, −52 kt w/w) but the export overhang is the standing cap on LME rallies.

HIKE RISK IS BACK ON THE TABLE INTO TODAY'S CPI: Fed Governor Waller said a hot core print would force the Fed to consider a NEAR-TERM HIKE. June consensus: headline −0.1% m/m (~3.9% y/y, flattered by June's ~10% gasoline slide — which July's +9.6% oil day has already reversed), core ~+0.2% m/m / 2.9% y/y. DXY ~101.2 and 10Y ~4.62% are already leaning hawkish — the dollar is the metals headwind today.
news.source: SMM morning minutes (14-Jul); IG CPI preview; Kiplinger; Trading Economics

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.2bn (−7% q/q); net income $425m ($1.60/sh); adj. EBITDA $595m; cash $1.4bn.
earnings.readthrough: Higher Al prices + operational resilience amid Gulf disruption; San Ciprián restart completed Apr 2026. 01-Jul: agreed to acquire South32's bauxite/alumina/aluminium portfolio for $4.1bn upfront (up to $5.6bn with contingent payments to 2030), close expected H1-2027. Q2 results THIS THURSDAY 16-Jul after the NYSE close (call 5pm EDT); Street consensus EPS $2.24 (vs $0.39 a year ago) on revenue ~$4.12bn — the first producer print assessed under the blockade regime (BusinessWire / AlphaStreet / Benzinga).

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due 22-Jul (07:00 CEST).

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.headline: Aluminium 835kt (+1% YoY); alumina +6%; bauxite −11% (Cyclone Narelle at Weipa/Gove); AP60 first hot metal.
earnings.readthrough: Flags Gulf curtailments = 23% of ex-China supply; expects enhanced 2026 deficit; Kitimat/AP60 ramp-up.

earnings.headline: Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady.
earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.headline: Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%).
earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings.headline: Guides H1-26 net profit ¥1.8–1.95bn, +154.4–175.6% YoY.
earnings.readthrough: Credits industry-chain cost reduction, aluminium prices fluctuating at highs, high-value-added deep-processing mix and energy-saving retrofits — the first sizeable China H1 pre-announcement of the season and a positive read-across for Chinese producer margins.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each listed peer's most recent reported results as of 14-Jul (public, listed-company information). Q2 season opens THIS WEEK: Alcoa Thu 16-Jul after the NYSE close — consensus EPS $2.24 on ~$4.12bn revenue (AlphaStreet/Benzinga) — Rio Tinto's Q2 production report is due mid-week (H1 results 13-Aug), Norsk Hydro 22-Jul (07:00 CEST), Maaden (1211) H1 late-Jul/early-Aug (Tadawul). Corporate action: Alcoa–South32 upstream transaction ($4.1bn upfront, up to $5.6bn contingent, close H1-2027). Context for the prints: Q2 average LME cash ≈ $3,459 (June) versus $3,138.5 at the latest official — sequentially strong realised prices with a fading tail. The two disclosures that matter most: what producers say about Gulf insurance/freight/toll costs, and about US Midwest premium realisation under the 50% Section-232 tariff.

commercial.text: The observable of the day: oil has priced the two-sided blockade (+9.6%, best since 2020) while the metal's first post-blockade official FELL and the spread snapped to contango — but that print pre-dates the 20% toll by ~4½ hours. Today's 13:20 London official and 13:30 CPI are the twin tests: a rebuilt backwardation + tame core validates catch-up toward $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382); a second contango + hot core (Waller: near-term hike) says the loose physical tape and the dollar still govern. $3,050 remains the structural line. Technical context, not advice.

Every premium print in the public data set (MJP spot $355 vs $395 settled; US MW DDP 109.75¢; EDU $542.5 mid) pre-dates the closure, the Omani-corridor strikes AND the toll. Friday 17-Jul's weeklies are the first assessed under the full regime — treat them as the real premium verdict. Mechanics to pre-compute: a 20% toll on strait cargo is a flat delivered-cost adder candidate for Gulf-origin premia in ALL destinations, and on the US route it stacks onto a tariff leg that is already ≈$1,569/t at the 13-Jul LME cash.

Alumina stays globally long (EGA ramping, FOB ~$340, API ~$304, China spot ~¥2,552) but Gulf-delivered costs deteriorate further: corridor missile risk + 20% toll on ~60% of regional alumina import routings. Secondary feed is the new tightener — the UAE's aluminium scrap export ban and EU tariff changes squeeze seaborne scrap while China's reverse-invoicing suspension cuts compliant domestic supply (SMM). Watch index-vs-delivered divergence on both alumina and scrap.

The pricing hierarchy this week: (1) war-risk quotes after a FATAL strike inside the 'safe' Omani lane — expect shorter validity and wider spreads immediately; (2) the 20% US toll — a policy cost whose contractual allocation (freight vs surcharge vs premium, CIF/FOB) is unsettled and will be negotiated cargo-by-cargo; (3) only then the assessed premia, which print Friday. Anyone quoting Gulf-origin delivered costs off last week's assessments is quoting a market that no longer exists.

bottom_line: On 14-Jul the market has its first honest disagreement of this phase of the crisis. Oil says the two-sided blockade — an Iranian closure declaration answered by a US blockade with a 20% cargo toll, missiles inside the Omani corridor, ~230 loaded tankers idling — is a supply event worth the biggest daily repricing since 2020. Aluminium's first post-blockade official said the opposite: cash fell 0.55% to $3,138.5 and the front spread snapped back to contango, because the metal's own physical evidence — China's June exports up 27%, premiums lower everywhere last week, EGA's alumina returning, off-season demand — is genuinely loose. Both can be right for a while; they cannot be right indefinitely with LME stock at 286,225 t and falling. The tie-breakers are dated: today's LME official (the first struck after the toll) and today's June CPI (where Waller has pre-committed the Fed to a hike discussion on a hot core); then Alcoa's Q2 on Thursday — the first producer read on war-freight and tariff realisation — and Friday's double: the Treasury's Iranian-oil deadline and the first premium weeklies assessed under blockade-plus-toll. Until those land, treat Monday's contango as the market's provisional verdict, not its final one.
sources: LME official settlements & stock

London Metal Exchange via Westmetall — westmetall.com. Latest PUBLISHED official is Mon 13-Jul (Al cash $3,138.50 / 3M $3,147.00 / stock 286,225 t); today's official prints 13:20 London, after this run. Series and June monthly means from the same table. US blockade + 20% strait toll (13-Jul)

Oil reaction — best day since 2020

CNBC — 'Brent oil jumps more than 9%, biggest daily gain since 2020, after Trump reinstates Iran blockade': cnbc.com/2026/07/12/oil-price-strait-hormuz-iran-trump-tanker.html. Brent settle $83.30 (+9.6%), WTI $78.14 (+9.4%); Kpler crossing counts and ~230 waiting tankers via TheStreet.

Shanghai Metals Market (SMM) Aluminum Morning Meeting Minutes, 14-Jul 09:08 Beijing — news.metal.com/newscontent/104002279. LME 3M close $3,158; SHFE 2608 ¥23,075 (+0.33%); ingot inventory 1.078 Mt (−52 kt w/w); UAE scrap export ban; Waller hike comment; US blockade start time.

GACC via CNBC — 'China exports in June jump at fastest pace since 2021': cnbc.com/2026/07/14/china-june-trade-data-exports-imports.html; SCMP (H1 +16.9%). Exports +27% y/y / imports +36%; June aluminium commodity detail pending at run time (May: 632 kt unwrought+semis, +15.5% — SMM/GACC).
MJP quarterly premium settlement
S&P Global Platts Q3-2026 MJP aluminium premium settled $395/t (assessed 06-Jul-2026), via IndexBox: indexbox.io/blog/japan-q3-2026-aluminum-premium-hits-decade-high-at-395mt/

Federal Register — 'Adjusting Imports of Aluminum and Steel into the United States': federalregister.gov/documents/2025/06/16/2025-11107/adjusting-imports-of-aluminum-and-steel-into-the-united-states
EU import duty & CBAM

Physical premiums, alumina, inventory

Shanghai Metals Market (SMM) — news.metal.com. Weekly prints of 10-Jul (Japan MJP spot $355/t; US MW DDP 109.75¢/lb) — ALL assessed before the 12–13-Jul escalation; domestic alumina spot weighted average ~¥2,552 (−61) as of 14-Jul; ex-China MG-alumina June output −6% y/y (SMM headline).
Macro / FX / energy reference

Trading Economics — tradingeconomics.com/commodity/brent-crude-oil ($83.37, +9.68%, 13-Jul settle; ~$83 Tue morning); tradingeconomics.com/commodity/aluminum (Al ~$3,150–3,200 Tue); DXY ~101.2, EUR 1.139, JPY 162.4, CNY 6.785, US10Y 4.62 (TE board, 14-Jul morning).
US June CPI preview (today 13:30 London)

IG — 'US CPI is out tomorrow' (13-Jul): ig.com/uk/trading-strategies/us-cpi-june-2026-preview-what-to-expect-tomorrow-260713; Kiplinger CPI preview; IndexBox CPI preview. Consensus: headline −0.1% m/m (~3.9% y/y from 4.2%), core +0.2% m/m (~2.9% y/y).
caveats: Report refreshed Tue 14-Jul-2026 ~06:35 London — BEFORE today's LME ring. The latest PUBLISHED official is Mon 13-Jul (cash $3,138.50 / 3M $3,147.00 / stock 286,225 t — Westmetall). Today's officials print 13:20 London, after this run. Monday's official was struck ~4½ hours BEFORE the US toll announcement (~17:53 London) — it carries the Iranian closure news but NOT the toll; the $3,158 close and ~$3,200 Asia prints are later, non-official references.

Base-metals board basis is MIXED at run time: the Aluminium row uses the 13-Jul official (day % = 13-Jul vs 10-Jul); Westmetall's public Cu/Ni/Zn/Pb pages still showed 10-Jul as latest when fetched, so those rows carry 10-Jul officials (day % = 10-Jul vs 09-Jul). YTD is vs 02-Jan officials throughout. Officials and closing prices are different series and are never mixed within a row.

Premiums, alumina and the overseas physical complex show the latest PUBLIC prints (SMM weekly 10-Jul; Platts marks 03/06-Jul) — ALL assessed BEFORE the 12-Jul closure declaration, the 13-Jul tanker strikes and the US toll. They therefore understate the current delivered-cost picture; Friday 17-Jul's weeklies are the first post-blockade assessments. Subscription indices (Platts/Fastmarkets) should be verified for any transaction.

Live-conflict reporting is fast-moving and contested: Iran disputes US control of the strait, the US disputes the closure, and casualty/damage details vary by outlet. The 20% toll's legal basis, collection mechanism and start date beyond the announced 16:00 ET enforcement are not yet documented in official gazettes. Sourcing reflects NPR, CNN, CNBC, UAE MoD statements and SMM as of run time. Elliott Wave content is technical context, not advice; probabilities are judgmental.
This report contains PUBLIC market information only. It is general market intelligence, not advice, and reflects no company's position, cost, volume or commercial data.

forward_path.basis: Latest PUBLISHED official (Westmetall); −0.55% vs 10-Jul — the first official after the IRGC closure declaration, struck ~4½ hours before the US toll announcement. Today's official (13:20 London) is the first to price the full two-sided regime.
Latest published official; −0.19% vs 10-Jul; front spread −$8.50 CONTANGO (back from +$3 backwardation). Later, non-official references: $3,158 LME close (SMM); ~$3,200 Asia (TE).

outlook.curve_note: The diagnostic answered — provisionally. Friday's +$3 backwardation did NOT widen on the first post-blockade official; it snapped back to a −$8.50 contango with stock at 286,225 t (−43.8% YTD). Read literally: the physical front end treated a declared closure as noise, because the metal's own tape is loose (China exports +27%, premiums lower, EGA returning). The caveat is sequencing — Monday's ring closed before the 20% toll existed. So the curve question rolls forward intact: if TODAY'S official rebuilds the backwardation, the physical market is repricing the two-sided blockade; a second contango print says supply repair still governs and the deferred curve's normalisation story (EGA ramp, China exports, Indonesia) holds the front in check. The 2026 consensus band ($3,200–3,500 averages) and the ~−365 kt structural deficit remain the floor beneath that argument.

consensus.note: Running above the World Bank base
First structural deficit in ~20 years; BofA flags near-term downside

balance.label: BofA/Morgan/BNP structural deficit; Goldman flags later surplus risk

capacity_pipeline.change: Alumina refinery RESTARTED 10-Jul (~50% within days, full capability end-2026); smelter rebuild ahead of schedule — 89 cells restarted
~19% capacity in controlled shutdown; host state was among those struck 12-Jul
Output near the 45 Mt self-imposed cap; exports running hot — June total exports +27% y/y (aluminium detail pending; May unwrought+semis +15.5%)
capacity_pipeline.timing: Structural ceiling on output; rising exports

capacity_pipeline.change: New smelter capacity ramping (BofA flags as added supply)
Incremental new/low-carbon capacity; Q2 production report due mid-week

scenarios.drivers: The two-sided blockade entrenches — the toll regime and Iranian strikes persist, the Omani corridor stays unsafe, and the metal closes its gap to oil on a sub-290kt stock base
drivers_list: Geopolitics: no Iranian 'strait open' statement and no Oman two-corridor agreement; the 20% toll is enforced from 16:00 ET 14-Jul and attacks on commercial tonnage continue — ~25% of ex-China supply stays hostage
Catch-up mechanics: oil +9.6% and freight repricing versus a metal official that FELL — if today's official rebuilds the backwardation, the same repricing arrives in metal with a lag
Inventory: 286,225 t (−43.8% YTD) and drawing daily — no visible buffer against a genuine export interruption from the Gulf
Premiums: Friday's first post-blockade weeklies print HIGHER on war-risk + toll pass-through, arresting the Q3 slide and re-anchoring Q4 MJP talks at/above $395
Targets: $3,400 fast on confirmation; Citi/Goldman ~$3,600; $4,000+ only on a sustained (30-day+) interdiction of Gulf loadings

scenarios.drivers: A managed-transit equilibrium emerges — the Oman two-corridor plan (or equivalent) keeps cargo moving at a higher cost, the toll is partially absorbed, and the loose physical tape caps the war premium
drivers_list: Geopolitics: Muscat-track diplomacy converges on managed transit (southern corridor free, northern under Iranian approval); sporadic incidents keep a risk premium in the front of the curve without stopping flow
Supply: the ~−365 kt 2026 deficit persists but repairs visibly — EGA alumina back, cells restarting, China exports +27% and Indonesia ramping
China: June trade blowout confirms the export overhang; four-year-high destocking (1.078 Mt, −52 kt w/w) supports the bottom; the import window stays shut
Macro: today's CPI lands near consensus (core ~2.9%) — no near-term hike, but no cut either; DXY ~101 caps rallies; the rates ceiling softens only if oil's spike proves transient
Premiums: weeklies firm modestly on war-risk/toll pass-through; Q4 MJP negotiates in the $340–410 band around the $395 anchor

scenarios.drivers: De-escalation or a hawkish shock — either a corridor deal + toll walk-back unwinds the war premium into accelerating supply, or a hot core CPI puts a Fed hike in play and the dollar does the damage
drivers_list: Geopolitics: the toll proves unenforceable or is negotiated away; an Iranian 'strait open' statement or an agreed Oman corridor plan restores flow — the June pattern (war premium unwinding in days) repeats
Macro: core CPI runs hot TODAY and Waller's near-term-hike scenario activates — DXY through 102, metals de-rate as a complex regardless of Hormuz
Supply: China's export machine (+27% June) keeps metal flowing into the off-season; EGA beats its ramp; premium weeklies print LOWER again despite the headlines
Physical confirmation: a second consecutive contango official confirms the front end never repriced the blockade at all
Technical: a daily close below $3,065 re-opens $2,990–3,020, then the .618 at $2,894

risks.risk: Two-sided Hormuz restriction entrenches (closure + US blockade + 20% toll on ~25% of ex-China supply routes)
Southern (Omani) corridor unusable after fatal missile strikes — no functioning workaround
Rapid de-escalation: Oman two-corridor deal / Iranian 'strait open' statement unwinds the war premium
Physical market is not short: China June exports +27%, premiums falling pre-blockade, EGA restart, off-season demand
Hot core CPI triggers Waller's near-term-hike path — dollar shock caps all dollar metals
US Section-232 at 50% + toll cost-push distort trade flows and US premium realisation

catalysts.event: US June CPI 13:30 London (consensus −0.1% m/m headline / core ~2.9%; Waller: hot core → near-term hike) + today's LME official 13:20 — the first struck under the full blockade-plus-toll regime; watch whether the backwardation rebuilds
US maritime blockade enforcement begins 16:00 ET; China June aluminium-specific customs detail expected (total exports already +27% y/y)
Rio Tinto Q2 production report (mid-week) — first upstream read on Gulf disruption effects on bauxite/alumina/aluminium volumes
Alcoa Q2 (after NYSE close; consensus EPS $2.24 / rev ~$4.12bn) — first producer print on war freight, insurance and US Midwest realisation under the 50% tariff
US Treasury Iranian-oil wind-down deadline; FIRST post-blockade premium weeklies (SMM/Platts) — the real premium verdict on closure + corridor strikes + toll

An agreed Oman two-corridor mechanism or an Iranian 'strait open' statement — the fastest unwind trigger; conversely, enforcement incidents at the 16:00 ET blockade start are the fastest escalation trigger

ai_analysis: 14-Jul's defining fact is a DISAGREEMENT between markets: oil booked its best day since 2020 (+9.6% to $83.30) on the blockade-plus-toll regime, while aluminium's first post-blockade official FELL 0.55% and the front spread snapped back to −$8.50 contango. One of these prices is wrong — or, more precisely, one of them is early.

The case that metal is lagging: Monday's ring closed ~4½ hours before the toll existed; since then the metal put in $3,158 at the close and ~$3,200 in Asia; stock is 286,225 t (−43.8% YTD); the 'safe' Omani lane took fatal missile hits; and Kpler shows strait crossings halved. Catch-up, if it comes, shows up first as a rebuilt backwardation on today's 13:20 official.

The case that metal is right: China's June exports beat consensus by nine points (+27%), every overseas premium printed lower last week, EGA's alumina is ramping back, seasonal demand is soft — and Waller has explicitly floated a NEAR-TERM HIKE if today's core CPI runs hot, which would hand the dollar the whip. An adequately supplied market with a hawkish Fed does not have to chase oil.

Resolution is dated and front-loaded: 13:20 official, 13:30 CPI, 16:00 ET blockade start today; Alcoa Thursday; the Treasury deadline and the first post-blockade premium weeklies Friday. Skew stays modestly up but the trigger discipline matters more than the view: backwardation + tame core = catch-up on; contango + hot core = fade the headlines. Technical context, not advice.

premium_explainer.desc: The physical-delivery adder over the LME price: regional logistics, duties/tariffs and local tightness — quoted $/t (or ¢/lb in the US) on top of LME cash. It is what converts an exchange price into a delivered price.

Q3-2026 settled at $395/t CIF Japan (Platts, 06-Jul) — a decade high and the fourth straight rise ($86 → $195 → $351.5 → $395). SMM's 10-Jul weekly had SPOT at $355 — below the settlement. That was assessed before the closure declaration, the tanker strikes in Omani waters and the US 20% toll; Q4 talks (~Sep) now open against a decade-high anchor and a two-sided chokepoint.

Platts duty-unpaid last publicly marked $525–560 (mid $542.50, 03-Jul), down ~$70 from mid-May as the war-spike unwound. The 6% EU duty, CBAM and Mozal's care & maintenance keep the structural floor — and the war-risk freight component has flipped from easing to re-widening, with a 20% US transit toll now stacked on top for strait cargo.

SMM's weekly DDP print eased to 109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul); Platts MW Transaction last printed 108.7¢ (03-Jul). The 50% Section-232 tariff-$ leg (≈$1,569 at the 13-Jul LME cash) dominates the all-in — US premia track Washington and the LME, not local tightness.

The decomposition below separates the mechanical tariff leg (Section-232 rate × LME) from the residual market premium. Most of the US 'premium' is a policy transfer, not a scarcity signal — and the new 20% strait toll extends the same logic to the freight leg of any Gulf-origin cargo, US-bound or not.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): Q3 settled $395 (decade high) but spot printed $355 before the blockade. The 17-Jul weekly is the first assessment to carry the closure, the tanker strikes AND the toll; if the two-sided restriction holds, the slide stalls and Q4 talks (~Sep) anchor at or above the settlement; a Muscat/Oman corridor deal reopens the path to the LOWER half of the $340–410 band.

European (EDU): last public Platts mark $525–560 (03-Jul). The 6% duty + CBAM floor plus Mozal's outage keep Europe structurally tighter than pre-war; the marginal driver — Gulf war-risk freight — is re-widening after missiles hit tankers INSIDE the Omani corridor, and the 20% US toll adds a new, non-negotiable cost line to strait-routed cargo.

US Midwest: SMM DDP 109.75¢/lb (10-Jul); the tariff leg (50% × LME ≈ $1,569) is ~two-thirds of the all-in, so US premia track the LME and Washington. Any blockade-driven LME gap lifts the US all-in mechanically — and Waller's near-term-hike signal cuts the other way via the dollar. Watch the tariff arithmetic and Friday's weekly, not the spot survey.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$68 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); oversupply persists — Metro Mining shipped a RECORD 1.8 Mt in Q2 (+7% YoY; 2026 guidance 6.6–7.1 Mt held; SMM 08-Jul); Guinea–EGA settlement returns CBG flows while Guinea weighs export quotas toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Mining.com/Discovery Alert)

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: surplus intact — EGA's restart ramps through H2 while SHFE AO sits below all moving averages; expect ex-China consolidation in the $300–340 band (FOB Australia ~$340, API ~$304, Platts futures ~$309). SMM's June ex-China output dip (−6% y/y) and Guinea's quota push are the counterweights to watch.

Bauxite: Guinea oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38); record Australian shipments add length. EGA's ramp is 'subject to optimisation of bauxite supply chains' — its buying returns demand to the seaborne market even as Guinea weighs a >$100/dmt export-price push.

Caustic soda / lime / freight: caustic stable (~$500/t FOB NE Asia). The delivered-cost leg IS the story: war-risk on Gulf-bound reagent and alumina freight re-widens after the Omani-corridor strikes, and the 20% US toll adds a flat policy cost to strait transits. The commodity is cheap; landing it at a Gulf refinery is not.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen. Energy complex repricing: Brent settled +9.6% at $83.30 (13-Jul) and EU TTF gas jumped ~8% to ~€53/MWh — gas-indexed smelter power costs follow with a lag. Rising carbon-cost exposure.

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-transparent leg of the input basket — Cu 3M $13,454.00 (10-Jul official, latest published at run time; the complex held steady through Monday's escalation while COMEX copper was flat). Busbar/cathode exposure prices off the LME.

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.val: Contested by BOTH sides — IRGC closure + US blockade & 20% toll
logistics.note: Iran's IRGC says the strait is closed 'until further notice'; on Monday the US reinstated 'the Iranian blockade' (barring Iranian ships/customers) and set a 20% toll on all other cargo, with the maritime blockade starting 16:00 ET 14-Jul. Kpler data show crossings down MORE THAN HALF week-on-week with ~230 loaded tankers waiting inside the Gulf. ~80–85% of Gulf aluminium exports and ~60% of regional alumina imports use this waterway (NPR / CNBC / TheStreet).

logistics.name: Southern (Omani) corridor
logistics.val: Attacked 13-Jul — no longer priced as safe
logistics.note: The JMIC had held the corridor 'operational'; hours later two UAE tankers (Mombasa, Al Bahiyah) were hit by Iranian cruise missiles INSIDE Omani territorial waters — one seafarer killed, eight injured (UAE MoD). Oman has drafted a two-corridor traffic plan (southern free; northern subject to Iranian approval) that is not yet agreed. Until it is, the last workaround carries live missile risk (BOE Report / TheStreet).

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.note: A fatal missile strike on commercial tonnage inside the 'safe' lane is the textbook trigger for wider war-risk quotes, re-activated exclusion clauses and shortened quote validity — and the 20% US toll (~$32m on a loaded VLCC at current prices, far above Iran's former ≤$2m charges) is a flat new policy cost on any strait transit. Both hit delivered costs before any assessed premium moves.

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: Latest public print 2,944 (+34) — a one-month high on Capesize momentum (Baltic Exchange via Hellenic Shipping News / Handybulk). BDI is rising on dry-bulk DEMAND (bauxite/alumina); the war-risk and toll components are separate, faster-moving cost lines. Price the legs separately.

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: With the strait contested by both navies, the southern corridor attacked and a per-transit toll in force, overland trucking to Red Sea ports moves from contingency to live logistics: carrier capacity, costings and lead times need to be current. This is a generic supply-chain observation for any Gulf-origin cargo.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: A declared closure plus a US toll regime is exactly the condition under which force-majeure clauses, laycan flexibility and WHO-BEARS-THE-TOLL questions (freight vs premium vs surcharge under CIF/FOB terms) get tested on Gulf-loading contracts. Generic contracting observation, not a position.

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (Brent settled +9.6% at $83.30, best day since 2020; EU TTF ~€53, +8% — gas-indexed power follows with a lag), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, 3M $13,454 at the latest published official).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.

Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China). New this week: the UAE implemented an aluminium SCRAP export ban and the EU raised scrap-relevant tariffs (SMM) — secondary-feed tightness is a rising cost-side theme.

Logistics is the input line that changed again: the commodity legs (alumina, bauxite) stay cheap, but the DELIVERED leg into and out of the Gulf now carries missile risk inside the 'safe' corridor plus a 20% US transit toll. Cheap alumina you cannot land — or can only land after a toll — is not cheap alumina.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): unchanged count — the five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) terminated at $3,855 on 02-Jun-2026 (an extended, war-driven fifth wave with confirmed bearish weekly RSI divergence), and the ABC correction (A $3,359 / B $3,406 / C $3,061.5 on 02-Jul) bottomed inside the $2,950–$3,080 confluence where the .50 retracement overlaps the prior fourth wave of lesser degree. All three cardinal rules remain intact, so no re-labelling is required. The recovery off $3,061.5 is now four ring sessions old and survived its first stress test: Monday's official — the first after the closure declaration — tagged the wave-two support shelf ($3,138.5 cash, within a dollar of the .50 retrace of the initial bounce at $3,138) and held on a settlement basis, though the front spread's snap back to contango withholds physical confirmation. Base case (50%): the corrective low is IN at $3,061.5 and a multi-month recovery builds toward $3,400–3,600 while weekly closes hold $3,050. Bull alternate (30%): the two-sided blockade (closure + US toll) forces the catch-up trade — a weekly close through $3,260 (.382) without further basing targets $3,700+. Bear alternate (20%): de-escalation or a Fed hike shock produces one final probe of the .618 at $2,894 before the larger recovery. Invalidation: a WEEKLY close below $2,850 voids the corrective count and re-opens $2,600. Confidence: moderate — the structure held its test, but the missing backwardation says smart money hasn't paid up yet. Technical context only, not advice.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Swing (daily): from the 02-Jul low at $3,061.5 the market built a five-wave micro-impulse into $3,210.5, corrected as a wave-ii to the .50 retrace at $3,138 (Friday's $3,139.5 close tagged it), and Monday delivered the retest: the first post-blockade official printed $3,138.5 cash — the .50 level to within a dollar — and HELD, before the tape recovered to $3,158 at the close and ~$3,200 in Asia once the 20% toll headline landed. Wave-ii is therefore confirmed at the $3,138–3,140 shelf on both a closing and an official basis, and the wave-iii thesis is engaged but UNCONFIRMED: the front spread's snap back to −$8.50 contango means the physical market has not yet paid for the story. Base case (50%): wave iii extends from the $3,140 shelf toward $3,230 (MA20) then $3,260 (.382 of the whole decline); a CLOSING break of $3,260 projects $3,330–3,400. Bull alternate (30%): today's official gaps through $3,200 on the toll regime and the backwardation rebuilds — the acceleration signature. Bear alternate (20%): a close back below $3,150 re-labels the bounce as a corrective B-wave and re-opens $3,065. Invalidation: a DAILY close below $3,050 breaks the swing count outright. Confidence: moderate — the fib discipline of this tape is exceptional (three consecutive levels tagged within dollars), but a wave iii that launches into a contango, a +27% China export print and a possible Fed hike is running against the fundamental grain; demand the spread's confirmation. Technical context, not advice.

premium_drivers.t: Hormuz is now blockaded from BOTH sides (the dominant driver)
premium_drivers.d: Iran's IRGC declared the strait closed 'until further notice' (12-Jul); on Monday President Trump reinstated 'THE IRANIAN BLOCKADE' — barring Iranian ships and customers — and set a 20% toll on all other cargo transiting the strait (~$32m for a supertanker vs Iran's previous charges of up to $2m). The US military said its maritime blockade begins 16:00 ET 14-Jul. Kpler counts strait crossings down more than half week-on-week with ~230 loaded tankers waiting inside the Gulf.
src: NPR — Trump says the US will reinstate its blockade on Iran and charge a toll on ships (13-Jul)

premium_drivers.d: Two UAE national tankers — Mombasa and Al Bahiyah — were hit by Iranian cruise missiles on 13-Jul while transiting the southern shipping lane INSIDE Omani territorial waters; one Indian crew member was killed and eight injured (UAE Ministry of Defence). The one functioning workaround is no longer being priced as safe: expect war-risk quotes to widen further and quote validity to shorten before any assessed premium moves.
src: BOE Report / Reuters — UAE says Iranian missiles struck oil tankers in Strait of Hormuz, one sailor killed (13-Jul)

premium_drivers.t: US Section-232 at 50% (the policy leg dominates the US number)
premium_drivers.d: The tariff-$ leg is ≈$1,569/t at the 13-Jul LME cash ($3,138.5 × 50%) — roughly two-thirds of the ~$2,420 US Midwest DDP all-in. US premia therefore track the LME and Washington far more than physical tightness; the 20% strait toll would stack a further policy cost onto Gulf-origin units.
src: Federal Register — Adjusting Imports of Aluminum and Steel into the United States (50% proclamation)

premium_drivers.t: EU: 6% duty + CBAM + lost regional supply
premium_drivers.d: The 6% import duty, CBAM carbon costs and Mozal (~580 ktpa) on care & maintenance keep a structural floor under Rotterdam premia even as the June war-spike unwinds; EDU was last publicly marked $525–560 (Platts, 03-Jul) — a level set before the blockade turned two-sided.
src: European Commission — Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism

premium_settlement_src: S&P Global Platts Q3-2026 MJP settlement $395/t (assessed 06-Jul-2026), via IndexBox

so_what.line: Hormuz is now a two-sided chokepoint — Iran calls it closed, Washington has reinstated an 'Iranian blockade' plus a 20% toll on all other cargo — and while oil had its best day since 2020 (+9.6% to $83), aluminium's first post-blockade official FELL 0.55% and the front spread snapped back to contango.
points: What moved: Brent settled +9.6% at $83.30 Monday (largest daily gain since May-2020) after Trump reinstated the blockade and set a 20% transit toll (~$32m for a supertanker), and Iranian cruise missiles hit two UAE tankers INSIDE Omani territorial waters — the 'safe' southern lane — killing one seafarer. The US maritime blockade formally starts 16:00 ET today.

Aluminium did not follow: Monday's LME official (the first struck after the closure declaration) printed $3,138.5 cash, DOWN 0.55%, and the cash–3M spread flipped back to −$8.50 CONTANGO from Friday's +$3 backwardation. Only after the ring — and after the toll headline — did the metal firm: $3,158 at the close, ~$3,200 in Asia this morning.

Why the divergence: the physical tape is adequately supplied — China's June exports surged 27% y/y (fastest since 2021; June aluminium detail pending after May's 632 kt), every overseas premium print last week was lower, and EGA's alumina restart adds supply. Policy risk is being priced in oil and freight first; the 20% toll is a delivered-cost shock that lands on Friday's premium weeklies before any LME repricing.

Watch today and this week: today's LME official (13:20 London) — does metal finally reprice, or is contango the verdict; US June CPI 13:30 London (consensus headline −0.1% m/m on cheap June gasoline, core ~2.9% — Waller says a hot core forces a near-term hike discussion); Alcoa Q2 Thursday (consensus EPS $2.24); Friday's Treasury Iranian-oil deadline plus the first post-blockade premium assessments.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (week of 15-19 June: cash $3,400.0, 3M $3,398.0, stock 315,300 t on 19 Jun); benchmark CFD price (~$3,400.85 on 19 Jun, ~+33% YoY), USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening, 17 June signing and 19 June Switzerland ceremony, and Thursday's 25-vessel transit via Al Jazeera, NBC News, PBS, Seatrade Maritime, CNN and the UK House of Commons Library (14-19 June); EGA/ALBA restart timelines and Gulf supply share via Trading Economics and Fastmarkets; MJP Q2 premium (~$350-353/t, +81% QoQ) and RUSAL China-to-Japan diversion via S&P Global Platts, Discovery Alert and AlCircle; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts.
weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
